data.title: Core CPI YoY Lowest Since March 2021, Stocks Record Highs, Key Charts
data.description:
The December 2025 CPI verified close to economists’ expectations, rising 0.3%. That brings the year-on-year rate to 2.7%, at estimates. Core CPI was cooler than expected, increasing by just 0.2%, holding the annual rate at 2.6%, which matches for the lowest since March 2021. It was the 58th straight month that the YoY CPI rate was above the Fed’s 2% target.

Carrying the numbers out an extra decimal place, the December headline CPI rose 0.31%, and core CPI increased 0.24%. Supercore CPI was +0.287%, taking the YoY rate to 2.741% (up from 2.703% in November). Real average hourly earnings are now +1.1% YoY, up from 0.8% in November.

Within the report, food prices climbed 0.7% from November, while energy CPI tallied a 0.3% sequential bump. Core goods inflation, key to tariff developments, was unchanged, helped by a 1.1% monthly dip in used vehicle prices. Apparel costs ticked up 0.6%, however.

Core services CPI added 0.3% last month, supported by a 0.4% shelter CPI rise and a 0.3% increase in rents. Hotel prices surged 3.5% in December, while vehicle insurance was a boon to commuters’ wallets, falling 0.4% in the year’s final month.

Equity futures initially rose, but then settled back once the numbers were digested. Treasury yields eased, with the benchmark 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) continuing to fail at the 4.20% key technical levels. Gold, silver, and bitcoin were all positive in the premarket.

December CPI Slightly Cooler Than Expectations

U.S. Core CPI YoY Lowest Since March 2021

0% Core Goods Inflation in December

CPI & Core CPI M/M and Y/Y

Supercore CPI Rose In December vs. November

CPI Categories YoY

CPI Using Real-Time Shelter Price Data is Under 2%

U.S. Truflation is Low at 1.74%

The 10-year Treasury Yield Was Rejected at 4.20% Again

IWM Hit Fresh Records Pre-Market

The VIX Dipped Under 15 Post-CPI

Looking ahead, inflation data continues rolling in this week. December PPI hits tomorrow morning, with key components feeding into the PCE Price Index, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge. Then tomorrow afternoon, the Fed’s Beige Book of regional business activity offers color on year-end sentiment and trends across the country.

Also keep your eye on upcoming January data points—the Philly Fed Index and the NY Empire Manufacturing Index are released at the same time as jobless claims Thursday morning. Industrial Production and the NAHB Housing Index arrive before the long weekend.

Data Calendar

U.S. Q4 GDP likely near 3.5%

The Bottom Line

Traders were not bracing for much CPI-related volatility. Indeed, the S&P 500 (SP500) was indicated unchanged after the data hit the tape, but small-cap Russell 2000 futures notched more record highs after the 8:30 a.m. ET report. I’m watching the 4.2% level on the 10-year Treasury, while bank earnings continue rolling in.

data.title: Celestica: When Engineering Becomes The Moat
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celestica Inc. (CLS) is not a low-margin contract assembler anymore but is instead a highly embedded engineering partner for the hyperscalers who develop AI data centers. This leadership position in high-bandwidth networking, fast time-to-production, and R&D-driven moat anchors a 28%+ revenue CAGR through 2028.

Since my first coverage, CLS is up 282%, and 26% since my last. With CCS programs scaling, 50%+ hyperscaler share, and a visible pipeline into 1.6T and 3.2T platforms, execution speed and incumbency make displacement increasingly unlikely.

Celestica Targets 28%+ Revenue CAGR Through 2028

Celestica’s primary bullish attribute is its expanding role as a high-end tech and design partner for hyperscale clients building AI data centers. There is a deeper integration that secures future top line and creates a competitive barrier. Celestica’s transformation began in 2018 when it was moving away from low-complexity programs. Celestica now focuses on high-performance data center tech. The results of this directional change are seen in the outlook and segment performance.

The Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segment (I see this as the epicenter of this activity) may grow its top-line by ~40% in 2026, and CLS’s management sees a path for at least 40% growth continuing into 2027. This outlook is built on specific high-value programs. For instance, Celestica expects its High-Performance Solutions portfolio to derive ~$5 billion in topline for 2025 alone (an 80% growth YoY). This growth is tied to direct business engagements.

The technical side of these engagements forms Celestica’s upside. Celestica employs over 1,100 design engineers and is increasing its research spending by at least 50% in 2026, directed at next-gen tech for AI workloads like 800-gig and 1.6T Ethernet switches, rack-scale liquid cooling, and custom ASIC AI compute. Also, Celestica collaborated on a first-of-its-kind rack-scale liquid-cooled 1.6T networking product for a hyperscaler.

Celestica focuses on design engineering and its capability to operationalize complex production lines. As per the management, after receiving initial silicon samples, Celestica produced an operational prototype for the 1.6T switch in a matter of days, and it is a process that takes competitors weeks. This speed and technical expertise in next-gen hardware are advantages for top-line growth. Why? This technical lead translates directly into market share. In the high-bandwidth Ethernet switching industry, the total market share of Celestica’s offerings in the combined 200-gig, 400-gig, and 800-gig platforms stands at 41%.

In the market segment for customized products for hyperscalers, Celestica's share of total spending is 55%. This market lead in the highest-performance tiers is self-reinforcing. As tech cycles shorten and complexity increases (moving from 800G to 1.6T and beyond), incumbents, and new entrants may find it difficult to match Celestica’s accumulated expertise and existing client integration.

Moreover, the forward top-line pipeline appears solid based on awarded programs. The 2026 top-line forecast of $16 billion is a central growth point. It is a 31% increase from the 2025 outlook. Beyond that, a major program with a digital native client for a rack-scale custom AI system may begin mass production in 2027. As per the management, this single engagement is worth at least a few billion dollars in the first year. For me, the visibility extends further as management confirms Celestica has already won follow-on programs for next-gen silicon that ramp into 2027 and is working on 3.2T tech for 2028 and beyond.

Overall, this combination of deep engineering integration with large data center clients, a lead in next-gen AI networking tech, big market share in its niche, and a visible multi-year pipeline of awarded high-value projects forms a bullish case for stock price growth. Celestica will directly benefit from the massive CapEx in AI infrastructure as seen in its topline growth estimates (28% CAGR 2025-2028).

Celestica’s Valuation Leaves No Margin

Celestica's stock price downside risk is in a valuation that appears to be pricing in years of flawless growth. CLS stock’s current metrics indicate that the Street has already credited Celestica for the immense growth outlined in its outlook. This is a situation where the bar for performance is set exceptionally high, and it is leaving little room for operational missteps and market shifts.

Celestica’s forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is 51x against a sector median of 25.8x. Its forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 33.7x, more than double the sector median of 15.7x. The forward Price/Book ratio is 15.3x, far above the sector median of 4.8x. These multiples mean that Celestica’s stock is priced at a massive premium relative to its sector peers.

However, as revenue scales from roughly $12 billion in 2025 to over $21 billion by 2027, the forward P/S ratio naturally compresses without requiring multiple expansion. What looks expensive on today’s sales becomes far more reasonable as top-line growth compounds at 30%+ annually.

CLS's stock price does not point to a company waiting for a turnaround, but it reflects a company that is expected to deliver exceptional growth without errors. This priced-for-perfection scenario is a direct risk to the CLS stock price. The above growth story that I detailed is precisely what fuels this high valuation. But because these optimistic forecasts are already at the current stock price of $302, any failure to meet these high expectations may trigger a sharp price correction in CLS stock. A quarterly miss on topline or EPS targets, or any downward revision to outlook, may disproportionately impact CLS stock.

Furthermore, Celestica’s business model adds another layer of risk. Celestica’s profitability, gross profit margin of 12.04% against a sector median of 48.55% is of a business with a heavy manufacturing and integration component where the cost of goods sold is very high. Celestica has improved its operating margin, but the low gross margin indicates limited pricing power on the total value of the products it sells. In a high-valuation view, this is a vulnerability. A small increase in component costs or unexpected pricing pressure from its large hyperscale clients may squeeze profitability, making it harder for Celestica to meet the high earnings expectations.

Finally, Celestica’s growth is highly concentrated on the CapEx cycles of a few large hyperscale and digital native clients. In Q3, three clients accounted for 30%, 15%, and 14% of the total top line. The outlook for 2026 and 2027 is also heavily dependent on the timely ramp-up of massive new programs. Any delay in a client's data center build-out, a shift in a client's technology choice, or a decision to dual-source a previously exclusive program may hit hard on Celestica’s capability to attain its growth targets. Under the current valuations, Street may not forgive such a setback. CLS stock’s 220%+ 1Y price return may not have discounted these concentration and execution risks completely.

Takeaway

Celestica is doing well from an operational standpoint, but it is already baked into sustained and flawless execution. With high multiples, gross margins are tight, and leverage to a few hyperscale cloud clients, the buffer to get by is very small. Just an earnings miss could result in a sharp revaluation.

data.title: Kinsale: A Quality Player In E&S Insurance
data.description:
Thesis

I believe Kinsale Capital (KNSL) is a buy because it is a long-term quality compounder that is consistently profitable and well positioned in a growing E&S market. Their profitability is supported by a significantly low expense ratio, growing their book value and enabling ROE far above their mid-teens target, the industry average, and peers in and above their weight class. Their loss ratios show consistent improvement, while their expense ratios are reducing fast enough to maintain their competitive lead beyond the near term.

Residual income modeling implies that KNSL is currently trading close to intrinsic value with minor overvaluation, using conservative figures. Relative valuation metrics suggest it is overpriced but are notably cheaper compared to their historical averages. Price has fallen by about 13.5% in the past year due to concerns related to premium growth, competition, and the softening market. Given strong financials, compounding book value, and operational efficiency, the price reduction presents an opportunity to enter.

Business Snapshot

Kinsale Capital Group is a mid-sized, pure-play specialty insurance company in the US P&C industry, founded in 2009 and headquartered in Richmond, Virginia. The breakdown of their gross written premiums [GWP] for the first 9 months of 2025 was “70.1% casualty and 29.9% property,” with a focus on small- and medium-sized accounts. They are authorized to sell in "all 50 states, the District of Columbia, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands" using their network of insurance brokers.

With 2024 year-end data, we can see that their top 3 states for total premium volume were California (18.8%), Florida (15.6%), and Texas (13.4%). The GWP breakdown by business lines is Commercial (97.4%) and Personal (2.6%). Their top 3 underwriting divisions were all in their commercial lines, and the breakdown by GWP was Commercial property (24.4%), Excess Casualty (13.1%), Small Business Casualty (10.5%). These rankings seem to remain the same for 2025 when looking at quarterly reports, although no specific annual breakdown is provided yet.

Recent Changes

There are concerns related to weakening growth in their Commercial Property division, increasing competition from large standard admitted carriers, and cyclical downturn from a softening market. These reasons are largely responsible for the price decline of about 13.5% in the past year as of Jan 4th. I believe each of these reasons are legitimate concerns but will not undermine its ability to produce above-industry-average excess returns beyond the near term. I plan to cover why they have a structural advantage that enables superior profitability and are a high-quality long-term compounder.

Structurally Supported Profitability

KNSL has structural advantages that enable superior profitability in the E&S industry. Through impressive operational efficiency and their technological platforms, they can maintain strong underwriting profitability across hard and soft markets. For example, on the graph below, we can see their combined ratio is consistently below their peers, as well as the wider P&C industry, by an average of about 18.5 points for the past 10 years. This impressive performance largely stems from their low expense ratios, which come from a level of efficiency that management considers a competitive advantage. Their expense management has been making marginal improvements since 2017. This is enabled by their "proprietary technology platform," which allows them to process a large amount of business without a proportional increase in cost. This works alongside their efficient underwriting and claims adjusting.

An example would be their "freedom of rate and form" by refusing to delegate their underwriting or claims of authority to third parties, which also alleviates costs while ensuring consistent and full control of their claims management and risk selection. Altogether, this gives them the ability to garner excess margins or the leeway to cut rates in a competitive market while still maintaining high profitability. Their structural efficiency also ensures they can sustainably generate value; e.g., for the first nine months of 2025, their annualized ROE was 29.1%, which is notably higher than the industry average of about 10%, their mid-teens target, and their peers.

Combined Ratio: KNSL vs. P&C Industry

Despite this, it can be argued that their superior underwriting profitability may not be durable in the coming years due to the slowing of premium growth and growing competition from standard carriers this past year. If this continues, KNSL's dominant position in the E&S industry may be threatened. To give an example, the growth in their gross written premiums has fallen from 42.3% in 2023 to 6.9% for the first nine months of 2025. This decline, in part, comes from the decline in their largest division, Commercial Property, seeing a reduction of 15.2% in premiums in the first 9 months of 2025 compared to the prior year's period. They've admitted this slowing growth is mainly due to "rate declines and an increasingly competitive environment" as standard carriers enter the market. If these competitive conditions progress, their low expense ratios may be insufficient to maintain their strong margins. Larger carriers, through scale and their diversified books, can take price rate cuts to gain market share from KNSL. All of this would work together to erode KNSL's margins, industry lead, and ability to produce excess returns. Despite ROE being high, we may already be seeing it normalize since their annualized ROE for the first nine months of 2025 decreased to 29.1% from 32.3% in the prior year's period.

However, this argument might be overlooking that KNSLs slowing premium growth in their current largest division is more reflective of their underwriting discipline and strategy rather than a weakening competitive position. Key metrics support that management is prioritizing profitability over volume and selective risk-taking in response to price competition from bigger players entering the E&S market. For example, despite worsening growth in their largest division, their combined ratio for the first nine months of 2024, which was 77.6%, did not get notably worse; for the same period in 2025, it was 77.5%. This shows good stability, as this result was maintained despite declining GWP growth and 2.3 points from net catastrophe losses due to the Palisades Fire, which was more than the 1.7 points of net catastrophe losses in the first nine months of 2024, largely due to hurricanes and tornadoes. We even see improving growth in other areas, e.g., during the first nine months of 2025, excluding their Commercial Property division, growth in GWP had increased by 14.4% compared to the same prior period thanks to their business seeing "strong submission flow from brokers across most divisions." This growth serves as a shield against competitors fighting on price in Commercial Property and highlights another strength of KNSL.

I believe a crucial reason KNSL sees strong submission growth is because of their speed of execution, which is enabled by their tech-based proprietary systems I mentioned earlier. It lets their "underwriters reply to the majority of submissions within 24 hours," allowing brokers to close deals faster. That's in addition to KNSL focusing on small- and medium-sized accounts, which are often overlooked to begin with by larger carriers. Altogether not engaging in a race-to-the-bottom pricing war, they protect their margins while rebalancing their portfolio of policies at prices they find acceptable for the expected risk. In the soft market, this is a better approach than forcing expansion and growth, which can cause adverse selection where they accept low-quality risks to preserve volume, which in the future may cause higher losses and hurt profitability and ROE. This assumes they maintain their underwriting discipline by remaining highly selective, which we've seen over the years since they accept roughly 5-7% of business submissions despite the submissions they receive steadily growing each year.

Quality Compounder

Slowing growth and competition may make us forget that KNSL is still a high-quality compounder in this space due to being a good capital allocator. This has been expressed primarily through underwriting discipline and secondarily through their growing investment income, which has allowed them to generate ROE in the mid-twenties across both soft and hard markets, unlike some peers. For example, I calculated 3-, 5-, and 10-year CAGRs (end period 31st December 2025) for Book Value, Net Premiums Earned, Net Income and Investment Income for KNSL, ACGL, MKL, and RLI. As can be seen in the table below, KNSL consistently ranked highest on these four criteria across those periods of time, which include hard markets (3Y CAGRs) and soft markets (10Y CAGRs). Seeing superior performance across hard and soft markets, we can infer that their returns are more driven by their ability to allocate capital than cyclical tailwinds.

Key Metrics CAGRS

Note: Some MKL 3Y CAGRs had a negative base year, hence the N/AI think the outperformance on these metrics reinforces the existence of structural advantages related to underwriting discipline and structural efficiency, based on their technology and systems. Their rapidly growing investment income suggests they are effectively reinvesting their float, which will allow earnings to multiply even if we see their underwriting growth normalize in the current market. We see this reflected in the growth of their net income and book value, and this is only strengthened by living in a healthy and growing E&S market. I believe all of these imply long-run price appreciation because for insurers, their intrinsic value is largely driven by book value compounding at high ROE across cycles, which we see here.

Underwriting Performance

I'd like to compare KNSL with peers to showcase how competitive they are in terms of their underwriting ratios. I'll compare them with other specialty insurers of varying sizes, such as ACGL, RLI, and MKL, to showcase KNSL's dominance. The combined ratio is the sum of their loss ratio and expense ratio. I'd like to focus on their combined ratio and expense ratios since their loss ratios are competitive but not enough to be a difference maker among their peers. They boast industry-leading combined ratios resulting in substantial margins. These are mostly due to their low expense ratio, which is persistently below their peers and the P&C industry.

As seen on the combined ratio graph below, KNSL frequently demonstrates a lower combined ratio than its peers. For example, on the graph below, since 2021 they have maintained a sub-80% combined ratio, and the persistent gap over the period 2013-2025 highlights the existence of their competitive advantage. This has remained true across soft and hard markets despite larger peers exerting scale-related advantages. This highlights that KNSL's advantage is more process-driven than scale-driven, suggesting they are not necessarily at a disadvantage if larger peers continue to enter the E&S market. Having stronger margins than others corroborates the compounding of their book value and mid-twenties ROE.

They have also been seeing steady improvements in margin since 2020 along with peers, but lately, we can see since 2024 KNSL, along with some peers, has remained flat or increased, likely due to softening market conditions. Since long-term insurer returns are strongly driven by underwriting margin, seeing that they can outperform peers by a consistent margin across different markets supports the fact that they are a high-quality long-term compounder. Just for a bit of clarification, the reason for their massive dip in 2015 was due to their reliance on a multi-line quota share (MLQS) reinsurance agreement where they ceded a large portion of their risk to reinsurers in exchange for large chunks of their GWPs. This had the effect of drastically lowering their loss and expense ratios in the years before 2016. This ended in 2016 after their IPO; hence their combined ratio normalized to the 75-85% range.

Combined Ratio: Peer Comparison

KNSL has a structural cost advantage due to their tech-based systems that enable low expense ratios. The graph below shows us that even after they ended their MLQS reinsurance agreement, their expense ratio normalized to about 25% in 2017. This was below the industry's expense ratio of 27% in 2017. Their annualized expense ratio for 2025 is 20.56%, which is below the industry's 25.2% as of mid-year 2025 and also meaningfully below their peers. Since 2017, they've maintained this advantage with incremental gains. Recently peers have experienced higher expense ratios alongside a softening market. Despite sharing the same conditions, KNSL has remained stable. Their margins and low costs allow them to compete on price at levels some peers would find difficult to maintain for extended periods of time, in exchange for sacrificing margin. As they grow, their lead may widen due to size-related advantages, strengthening the case for their expenses not rising in proportion to their GWP due to their cost advantage. This shows how KNSL can maintain superior underwriting margins and manage their mid-20s ROE across market cycles, even as their premiums compound at rates greater than competitors, again reinforcing why they are a high-quality insurer among their peers.

Expense Ratio: Peer Comparison

Valuation

Multi-Stage Residual Income Model

A multi-stage residual income model (RIM) suggests that the mean intrinsic value per share of KNSL is $379.80. The stock price as of the 12th of Jan 2026 is $397, suggesting the stock is modestly overvalued at 4.3%, leaving them close to fair value. This presents investors with a chance to build or add to a position in a wonderful company at a roughly fair price, given its fundamentals.

Estimated Intrinsic Value Range

The intrinsic value per share is the sum of the current book value per share, the present value of residual income, and the present value of the terminal value. The residual income is the difference between net income and the minimum required return an investor expects for each forecast year. The terminal value is the sum of residual income earned in perpetuity from year 15 onwards. The book value per share is the assets minus liabilities, i.e., shareholders' equity, on a per-share basis.

Composition of Intrinsic Value:

An investor's required minimum return, aka cost of equity, is calculated using the CAPM formula, Ra=Rrf+[Ba x (Rm-Rrf)]. This formula uses the risk-free rate, equity risk premium, and the beta. The risk-free rate used is the US 10-year treasury yield of 4.175% as of Jan 2026. The US equity risk premium of 5, as of Sep 2025, is the extra return an investor expects for accepting the risks of equities over the risk-free asset. The beta of 1 tells us how volatile a stock is relative to the market. Greater than one means it is more volatile than the market and vice versa. If it is 1, then it moves in perfect sync with the market on average. Inputting these values gives me the cost of equity of 9.18%, which will be used to discount the residual income and terminal value to get their present values.

CAPM Inputs

I chose a persistence factor of 0.59; this is the decay rate of the excess returns. It corresponds to a narrow and stable moat, which I think reflects their underwriting discipline and operational efficiency in their space. The persistence factor does not have a significant effect on the intrinsic value. I set the dividend payout ratio to half in 5 years from now, which would be 1.89%; it also doesn't have a critical impact on the intrinsic value. Their dividend yield is not an attractive part of this investment since its 4-year average dividend yield is 0.16%, much lower than the sector median of 3.35%.

Out of these assumptions, the terminal ROE has the greatest impact on the intrinsic value estimate; I set it at the P&C industry ROE of 10%. A 10% terminal ROE means I assume ROE steadily declines from its mid-20s to 10% across a 15-year horizon. This implies that the firm's ability to create value normalizes the industry average over time. I wanted to use conservative figures to see if a mean undervaluation or fair valuation could be identified through this method. I think this is corroborated by management's authorization of a $250M share repurchase program in December 2025, suggesting it is reasonable compared to its intrinsic value

Relative Measures

Relative measures also confirm an overvaluation of the financial sector but a meaningful discount compared to historical averages. For example, their P/E GAAP (FWD) is 19.11x, which is 45% more expensive than the sector but about 43% cheaper than its 5-year average. Similarly, its P/B (FWD) is at 4.71x, which is much higher than the sector median at 1.25x but about 33% cheaper than its 5-year average. I think the market recognizes KNSL for its superior margins in the space and highly values its book. It doesn't usually trade around fair value; it has historically traded higher. So, by waiting for it to be undervalued by average insurer standards, one might miss the chance to enter when it's around fair value despite its above-average performance. Given the current pullback KNSL is experiencing, some may prefer to use dollar cost averaging (DCA) to build a position to take advantage of the pullback. To clarify, this is under the expectation that it will eventually rebound once the market realizes they are fundamentally strong despite the growth and cyclical changes.

Risks

Pricing and Competition: As we see the market softening, larger competitors are entering the E&S space and are chasing volume by competing on price. As this continues into 2026, it will challenge KNSL's growth and market share across key divisions like Commercial Property. We may see KNSL either proactively shrink to maintain margins, which I think is more likely and in line with their underwriting discipline, or sacrifice those margins if they deem it necessary to maintain a certain level of volume in key divisions.

Inflationary Pressures: Trade and tariff volatility introduce inflationary risk, which can result in adverse developments to their loss reserve adequacy. These pressures can affect claims cost estimates and have an adverse effect on reserve adequacy in the following years as well. For example, in Q1 2025, their overall "favorable development was offset in part by adverse development primarily from the 2017 through 2019 accident years."

Catalysts

Growing E&S Market: KNSL is well positioned as a pure-play specialty insurer in a growing part of the industry, which has seen some moderation in 2025. E&S has been seeing double-digit premium growth these past few years, with their participants experiencing "more favorable underwriting results and greater top-line growth" than the wider P&C industry.

Technology and Cost Advantage: KNSL's platforms function as a digital moat that enables speed and lower operating costs in their niche. This advantage has shown incremental improvements reflected in the expense ratio. This also provides leverage as they scale with incrementally increasing premiums, requiring minimal overhead for small and high-margin accounts that competitors may overlook. This cost discipline allows them to protect margins from a growing competitive landscape even as growth moderates.

Conclusion

In conclusion, KNSL's above-average ROE is enabled by their underwriting margins and operational efficiency, powered by their proprietary technology platform and freedom of rate and form. This enables them to maintain competitive loss ratios while having expense ratios much lower than peers and the P&C industry, allowing them to be substantially profitable across market cycles. This is in addition to absorbing large catastrophe losses each year, which showcases their resilience.

Residual income modelling suggests they are trading near fair prices while being at a rare discount to their historical averages. Recent concerns surrounding growth, competition, and cyclical change have resulted in a pullback despite underwriting quality remaining strong. This may present a good opportunity to build or add to a position in KNSL, such as through (DCA), to take advantage of a pullback, assuming an eventual rebound given their fundamentals and resilience.

data.title: December CPI Report: Inflation Meets Expectations As Gasoline Declines
data.description:
Throughout 2025, inflation continued to track in a narrow range, higher than the Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) targeted range but within a window not seen as overly concerning from an investor perspective. This inflation trend continued into December, as seen in today’s release by the Labor Department.

Ahead of the release, equity markets have tracked mostly higher to start the new calendar year. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), S&P 500 (SPY), and Nasdaq (NDX) have all traded near record territory, with the DJI still near the 50,000 milestone, despite slight pullbacks yesterday following the news of the Trump administration’s investigation of Fed Chairman Jerome Powell.

Equities were mostly flat on Tuesday prior to the release of the December CPI report, which landed largely within expectations. In the bond markets, yields on the 10-YR Treasury (US10Y) increased to the 4.2% mark, perhaps due to the implications relating to the investigations.

While I don’t believe today’s data release will impact the Fed’s rate decision later this month, I do believe the Fed’s forward path has been made more complicated by recent events. Here’s everything you need to know about the December CPI release.

Where Did Prices Increase in December?

The headline rate rose 2.7% YOY in December, the Labor Department reported on Tuesday. This was in line with expectations and consistent with the pace seen in November.

Gasoline prices declined 0.5% during the month and are now down 3% over the past twelve months. The declines in gasoline prices over the past year have been a boon for both travel and consumer wallets to a degree. With less to spend on gasoline, consumers have been able to offset some of the increases seen elsewhere in the CPI report.

Gas prices have followed a welcome path to start the new year, with the national average hitting their lowest levels since 2021. According to AAA’s most recent reporting, the national average price of a gallon is $2.81, down from above $3.00/gallon at the same time last year. This should translate to a continued tailwind into upcoming CPI releases, in my view.

The declines in gasoline were offset by increases in the food index. During the month, the food index rose 0.7%, with a 0.7% increase in both the food at home and food away from home categories. Driving the index during the month were increases in the cereals and bakery products index, which increased 0.6% during the month.

What Core Categories Increased or Decreased in December?

During the month, the core categories also increased about 2.6% YOY, with an increase of 0.2% on the month. The increases were driven by the familiar categories of shelter and insurance, each of which rose 0.4% and 0.4% during the month.

Car buyers received a reprieve in December, with used vehicle prices declining by 1.1% during the month. Though down, the category is still up 1.6% YOY. The new vehicle index also held flat, with YOY increases of 0.3%.

Fed Policy Implications

With the December CPI release landing mostly in line with expectations, I don’t believe the data will materially change what the Fed does at this month’s meeting. The CME FedWatch Tool, for example, is showing a 95% chance that the Fed will keep rates at its current benchmark range in January, and I believe this is likely what is to occur.

In prior analysis, I’ve noted that a steady hand remains the base case. In my commentary, I’ve noted that inflation continues to track in a narrow range, not too cool and not hot enough to force the Fed’s hand in the other direction. In isolation, the December CPI report fits neatly into a “wait-and-see” framework.

Where things get far more complicated is the forward path, specifically because of the Trump administration’s investigation into Powell. Markets are increasingly being asked to price not just inflation dynamics, but political risk embedded directly into monetary policy.

The investigation itself may not alter near-term rate decisions, but it sends a loud message about the pressure future Fed leadership could face to align policy with presidential preferences, and that matters a lot, in my view.

Fed independence has long been a cornerstone of market stability, anchoring inflation expectations and long-term rates. I believe that undermining that credibility, even implicitly, raises questions about whether future policy decisions will be driven by data or political expediency. We saw a glimpse yesterday following the news. A weaker dollar, a jump in gold, and rising long-end Treasury yields suggest investors are beginning to demand a higher risk premium.

Ultimately, while today’s CPI report landed largely in-line with expectations, the politics around the Fed are anything but, and that’s where uncertainty is quietly building.

Final Thoughts on December CPI Report

On the surface, December’s CPI report was about as uneventful as investors could have hoped for. Both headline and core inflation landed largely squarely within expectations, and this came at the same time that quarterly earnings season began to kick off in earnest.

Within the print, familiar pressures, namely shelter and insurance, continued to command the most weight, while lower gasoline prices remained a meaningful offset for consumers. That dynamic has been in place for months now, and December did little to challenge that narrative.

What did change, however, was the setting against which this data is being interpreted. While I don’t expect today’s CPI release to alter the Fed’s decision to likely hold rates steady later this month, the outlook beyond January feels noticeably less certain.

I believe that the Trump administration’s investigation into Powell will contribute a new variable to markets in the form of political risk inside the Fed itself. We saw an early reaction to that uncertainty yesterday, with equities stalling, long-term yields drifting higher, the dollar weakening, and gold catching a further bid.

With equity markets trading at elevated valuations and risk premiums beginning to reprice, I continue to view a more defensive posture as prudent. Inflation may be stable for now, but confidence in the Fed’s independence is no longer being taken for granted, and I believe that could be a far bigger issue than any single CPI print.

data.title: Micron: Follow Insider And Trim Ahead Of Heavy Capital Outflows
data.description:
Micron Outshone My Expectations

For months, I had been bullish on Micron's (MU) prospects when the market seemed indifferent. However, when the stock started to recover and its valuation grew faster than I initially expected, I decided to downgrade to "Hold". That was a wrong decision, or at least a premature one, as MU added ~79% to its market cap since that downgrade of mine, while the market hasn't moved up even by 5%. It looks like Micron is gaining much more attention from ordinary investors than it did just a few months ago. Although it closed 2025 with a 237% gain and is up over 17% YTD (in less than 2 weeks of January 2026), most of its valuation metrics are still looking quite cheap. This can't help but attract value and GARP-minded investors looking for an outperformer of 2026.

While I actually agree that MU may be in a different situation now compared to its previous cycles, I'm calling for caution regarding its low multiples because they tend to signal a cyclical peak rather than an entry point right now, in my view. Insiders have sold over $73 million worth of their shares over the past 3 months, and while they might have done so for their own reasons (not related to the fundamentals), I think trimming at above $340/share levels should be the right choice. I like the financials so far, but again, if we're close to a peak in pricing, MU's margins can drop and hurt those buying the stock at its ATHs now. That's why I'm leaving my "Hold" unchanged, hoping for a repricing to the downside and fresh factual data from Micron (they're reporting in about 2 months).

My Updated Reasoning For A 'Hold'

I completely understand the change in narrative that memory is now viewed as the primary bottleneck for AI and everything tied to it. The reason for MU's rise lies in memory scarcity - their HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) capacity is completely sold out for the 2026 calendar year at above-anticipated pricing (up to 70% in some segments), which leads to superior margins and hence leads to a higher stock price. It's an industry-wide tendency - Micron's key peers, such as Samsung and SK Hynix, are also discussing price hikes of up to 70% for server memory, according to TechSpot's sources.

Speaking particularly about Micron, the areas where it stands out are tech leadership and customization moats.

On the one hand, they're leading with their 1-beta DRAM and G9 NAND nodes while preparing to roll out HBM4, which features a 2048-bit interface and speeds exceeding 2.0 TB/s. After a likely ramp in Q1 FY2026, Micron's HBM4 will power Nvidia's (NVDA) Rubin platform and hence get even better pricing and market standing.

On the other hand, Micron is shifting toward HBM4Es, which are basically custom-made HBMs that allow MU to integrate logic directly into the memory stack via partnerships with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM). That's why the bulls see this cycle differently from what we could have seen before - HBM4Es aren't commodities anymore but co-processors, which can have higher margins for much longer.

While I can't argue directly against the above points, I see some flags that support a more cautious approach.

First, I don't like the executives' selling activity. This factor was brought to my attention by another Seeking Alpha analyst, Envision Research, who wrote the following:

In the past three months, there have been 54 total sell transactions disclosed, amounting to a total value of more than $73 million. Latest individuals who sold included President and CEO Sanjay Mehrotra, EVP and Chief People Officer April S. Arnzen, and CVP and Chief Accounting Officer Scott R. Allen.

Source: Envision Research's article on MU

Indeed, if we go to the NASDAQ website, we'll notice that over the past 3 months alone, the management has sold almost half of the amount sold for the past 12 months. It doesn't look bullish to me at all.

Again, insiders sell for multiple reasons, and insider selling itself isn't the main thing that should influence our investment decisions. But it's taking place right at the stock's ATH.

Plus, Micron is currently increasing its fiscal 2026 CapEx to $20 billion while planning to invest ~$100 billion over the next 20+ years to construct a new megafab in New York. The onshoring process is clearly pushing Micron to increase its overall capital intensity in the coming years, and if the AI supercycle experiences even a minor cooling from today's levels, I think Micron may not achieve the ROICs it's targeting from this initiative. What if the current supercycle follows the path of the past ones? Even if it takes another 1-2 years for that to happen, currently high barriers to entry should eventually get low enough to allow new supply to press pricing power and Micron's margins.

I've seen a lot of discussions lately that the current memory shortages won't improve before 2028, even amid the massive investments. Assuming that MU's gross margin takes another leg higher, I don't see any stabilization above the 60% level anyway after 2028.

And knowing that the market might want to reflect such kinds of things in advance, I think Micron might register a few new ATHs from here, but it'll likely face heavy profit-taking once the above trend becomes more obvious to the market participants. I can only guess here, but what if that's the reason for the insiders' selling? They understand these cycles best, and that may be why they are quietly heading for the exits, while ordinary investors risk getting trapped by buying into a stock trading at ATHs just as the company commits to decades of heavy capital outflows.

Now, let's take a closer look at Micron's valuation. It does look cheap right now, as MU trades at 10.64x forward earnings, and it's valued for only 8.60x FY2027 EPS, according to Seeking Alpha.

It looks cheap, but we have to understand why we see low P/Es here. The market is telling us a very simple thing: Micron's earnings power isn't projected to stay reliable beyond 2027. The memory chip industry is highly cyclical; as we approach peaks, firms constantly scale capacity to chase premium pricing, which eventually leads to oversupply just as demand falls or stalls. Then, pricing weakens, and industry players see their revenues drop. That's exactly why investors are not paying more than 8-9x earnings on FY2027 EPS - what comes in FY2028/29 isn't about growth; it's about surviving.

After the massive earnings revisions we've seen recently, I don't think Micron will be able to exceed its usual track record of average beats next year. Even if MU's P/E multiple rises to 10x next year, the upside would be capped at 16-17%. In my opinion, the bulk of the profits in Micron stock have already been made; even if pricing and volumes rise from here, they are unlikely to provide sustainable returns for incoming investors.

Your Takeaway

I don't question MU's reasons for the past year's rally and even for the rally that we've been seeing since the beginning of 2026. But everything has its natural limits.

In my opinion, Micron is poised for at least a healthy correction from today's levels. In my understanding, the current narrative should get a reality check on the memory market's conditions and needs in the next few weeks, and Micron's rally should cool off, meaning-reversing.

I don't like the recent insider selling activity and the seeming consensus that Micron's cycle is different this time. Much of the future financial performance seems to be perfectly reflected in the price action, so the upside from here is capped at best. We need to see a correction before any meaningful opportunities for MU buyers arise. I remain neutral, willing to upgrade only if Micron contracts from here.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: 2026 Sustainable Investment Trends
data.description:
Sustainability themes continue to impact the investment landscape and offer major opportunities as well as risks. In this report we highlight some of the key sustainability trends for investors to consider this year: from physical climate risk to the energy transition, AI, Health Care, Food Producers and regional markets.

Introduction

Sustainability themes continue to impact the investment landscape and the next wave of investment opportunities will be driven by the economic and investment benefits these themes can deliver.

As markets evolve and against an unstable geopolitical backdrop, sustainability issues are becoming ever more important. With the physical effects of climate change already costing hundreds of billions of dollars per year, the energy transition driving a multi-trillion-dollar green economy and artificial intelligence (“AI”) offering enormous opportunities as well as risks, sustainable investment will remain a key investment theme in 2026. In this report, we highlight key sustainability trends for investors to consider this year: from physical climate risk to the energy transition, AI, Health Care, Food Producers and key regional markets such as Asia.

At a glance

  • Physical climate risk will increasingly present rising costs and impacts on lives, livelihoods and economies. Climate adaptation and resilience, alongside transition, will be in focus for investors, corporates and governments.
  • The global energy transition, from renewables to energy efficiency, will continue to grow rapidly. Subsidy changes in China and US will be felt, but growth is expected to continue, given underlying electricity demand, low cost and speed/scalability.
  • As the global AI data centre build out continues, access to power remains critical. As we move into an operational “show me” period, the impact of efficiency on costs and productivity, as well as the delivery of end user process optimisation and emissions reduction, will be in increasing focus.
  • A shift to Asia for climate and sustainability in 2026, with a focus on China’s delivery of its emission-cutting pledge and the country’s dominant position in the clean energy market. India will become an increasingly important ‘swing factor’ for the direction of climate policy.
  • Beyond climate, critical areas such as Health Care and Food Producers have seen cost pressures, regulatory challenges and poor investment performance in recent years. As large, geopolitically essential sectors with strong exposure to demographic drivers and a lot of underlying innovation they should remain in focus for sustainable investors in 2026.

Sustainable Investment trends in 2026 - overview

Trends

Physical Climate Risk

“It’s happening”

Physical climate risk is already presenting real costs and challenges to governments, corporates and investors, underlining the critical impact of climate change happening earlier than expected. With temperature anomalies now above 1.5 degrees compared to the pre-industrial level, the cost of physical climate risk remains high: it is estimated at $220bn for 2025, with an estimated $28.3trn of GDP at risk by 2050. This is driving a growing push for resilience and adaptation.

Energy Transition

“It’s still happening”

The energy transition is set to continue at pace in 2026, despite geopolitical headwinds in the form of tariffs and subsidy changes. Demand for electricity is growing rapidly, with renewables (particularly solar) the main source of growth. There is also significant interest in areas such as nuclear, grid and storage infrastructure and energy efficiency. However, geographical differences are affecting the energy transition, with trade tensions and profitability challenges along the value chain affecting the pace of change. There are also some speed bumps from changing support mechanisms in 2025, which will be felt in 2026. The fossil energy market is already seeing an impact, particularly in oil demand.

Asia

“It’s happening here”

The epicentre of current climate and sustainability activity and investment is shifting towards Asia. China’s emission cuts pledge has the potential to slow the pace of reduction in carbon emissions globally: 2025 saw the beginnings of small coal power generation cuts, but the market will be looking for more action in 2026. China continues to dominate both the clean energy generation and manufacturing sectors, has made massive strides in the electrification of transport and is seeing significant rises in green bond issuance. India is becoming the new swing factor in global emissions, with rapid economic growth and energy demand, but also an increasing renewables share and significant climate issues, ranging from urban air pollution to an increasing number of high heat days. The path India chooses to power its future economic growth is likely to have a significant impact on the global climate. Japan’s GX transition policy clearly links emissions cuts to boosting efficiency and to growth in energy security.

Technology

“A plethora of positives and negatives”

The AI and associated data centre gold rush is propelling global investment activity, with a scramble for energy and expectations of economy-wide efficiency gains. 2026 will become a year where the industry needs to delivery returns, efficiency and outcomes for users. In addition, lingering concerns around cybersecurity, governance and equity market exposure to technology will continue in 2026.

Health Care

“Overlooked $10 trillion SDG”

Health Care has a massive impact on both economies (costing around 10% of GDP in a world of indebted governments) and lives. It has been heavily impacted by rising costs and is seeing significant innovation (GLP1s, gene editing, AI Health Care, EM patients and producers, demographics). However, Health Care has been one of the worst-performing ICB Industries in the FTSE All-World index over 5 years and is often overlooked by both mainstream and sustainable investors.

Food Producers

“Will food security be the new energy security?”

Food production is a multi-trillion-dollar industry, but hundreds of millions still face hunger. Food price inflation has outstripped broader inflation measures and food security is becoming an increasingly important geopolitical risk factor. The combined impact of biodiversity, climate change, demographics and urbanisation could propel the topic to the forefront in 2026.

Physical Climate Risk – it’s happening

The increasing evidence of accelerating physical climate risk continues to underscore the systemic impact of climate change.

Global temperature anomalies are already over 1.5 degrees 1 and physical climate risk presents significant costs to the global economy (an estimated $220bn 2 in 2025, albeit below the 2024 figure). In 2025 alone there have been wildfires in US and Europe, flooding in Pakistan and South Africa, heatwaves in India and hurricanes and cyclones across the Americas and Asia. The summer heatwave in Europe is estimated to have cost ~0.5% of GDP 3, a material figure in a time of slow growth.

Figure 1: GDP facing high exposure to physical climate hazards ($ trillion)

Source: COP30 Net Zero Atlas | LSEG.

An estimated 839 million people and $28.3trn of GDP are at risk from physical climate impacts by 2050 4. The risk comes from multiple sources, be it heatwaves, cyclones or water stress. These risks will increasingly manifest in highly populated areas and major cities, placing additional costs on already highly indebted governments. In a world searching for an economic growth, heatwaves will negatively impact productivity. Ageing infrastructure is already being impacted by weather issues: examples are low water levels in the Panama Canal in 2023 or heat waves impacting power grids 5. This increases the infrastructure deficit in both developed and emerging markets and raises the costs of closing it. Increasingly, investors will need to pay attention to the potential impact of physical risks across a range of asset classes: the impact of wildfires on Los Angeles municipal bonds is a recent example. Our latest sustainable investor asset owner survey 6 found that 85% of respondents rated climate concerns at 7 out of 10 or higher, while 80% of respondents now incorporate climate or sustainability considerations (often via indices) into their strategic asset allocation.

Growing physical climate risk (and a growing investor focus on it) are also increasing interest in adaptation, long the poor cousin to transition. 34% of large and medium corporates state they are incorporating adaptation measures into their climate transition plans. A third of green bonds have some form of adaptation in their use of proceeds. It is also stimulating the growth of the already $1 trillion adaptation solutions market. Adaptation in emerging markets will also be a key area, given the severity of physical impacts already being seen there and the commitment in COP30 to triple the funding of support for adaptation measures.

Figure 2: Share of FTSE All World constituents citing adaptation measures in their corporate disclosures

European regulators are also compelling financial institutions to consider climate adaptation and resilience, despite some roll-back of similar climate disclosure requirements in the US. The latest Climate Financial Risk Forum (CFRF) guidance 7 from the Bank of England and Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) emphasises that physical climate risks are now financially material, requiring firms to move from high-level strategies to operational resilience. Climate resilience is shifting from a compliance issue to a value driver. Practical frameworks and scenario analysis will increasingly be used to allocate capital to resilience-enhancing assets and to position better for long-term stability and regulatory alignment.

With growing physical climate risk manifesting itself across the global economy, climate considerations are now integral to the financial markets--whether it’s investing in transition, adaptation, building new infrastructure or increasing insurance.

Energy Transition – still happening

The global energy transition is continuing to progress at pace, despite geopolitical headwinds and changes in carbon emissions so far not hitting the rates required for a net zero trajectory.

2025 has been another year of strong growth in the production of renewable energy, with the first 3 quarters adding 621 terawatt hours (TWh). This increase was dominated by solar and outpaced total energy demand growth of 603 TWh, while fossil fuel generation saw a slight contraction of 17 TWh 8. These trends are expected to continue in 2026, underpinned by a continued electrification of energy demand and the low cost, scalability and energy security benefits of renewables, over and above any environmental benefits.

Between 2025-2030 global renewable power capacity is projected to increase by 4,600 GW, equal to the installed capacity of China, EU and Japan, with around 80% of this total coming from solar PV 9. Global electricity demand is expected to grow by ~3.7% in 2026, faster than GDP and ~twice as fast as overall energy demand, driven by AI, electrified transport, industrials and heat/cooling demand 10.

Figure 3: Changes in electricity generation by technology and demand, Q1-Q3 2025 vs 2024 (TWh)

Source: Q3 Global Power Report: No fossil fuel growth expected in 2025 | Ember.

However, it is important to keep in mind factors which may create a speed bump in 2026. The phasing out of renewables subsidies by the US in late 2025 brought forward demand, which will likely weaken in 2026. China removed its renewable energy feed-in tariff scheme in June 2025 and introduced so-called “anti-involution” measures to combat the current weak profitability in Chinese new 11 12 energy companies (a result of overcapacity, intense competition and aggressive price-cutting). However, these developments are expected to be balanced out by growth elsewhere, such as increased renewables demand from emerging market countries or regions such as India, ASEAN or Africa, driven by low cost, scalability and energy security. The net change in the IEA’s estimate for 2025-30 renewable energy growth from 2024 to 2025 is only down 5%, a relatively small change in the context of the overall predicted rise.

Despite the spectacular performance of solar (an estimated 626GW of new solar capacity in 2025, 4 times that installed in 2021 and equivalent to around half of all generation capacity in the US 13), other technologies and investment areas are gaining in focus. Nuclear power is attracting interest from big tech as a source of data centre power, there are plans to restart reactors in Japan and the US and there is excitement about the potential for new nuclear technologies such as Small Modular Reactors (SMR): 127 SMR technologies are 14 and some are expected to be in commercial operation in the next 5 years. Investor interest in nuclear is increasing with the FTSE Global Nuclear Power Index 15 returning 63% in 2025, twice the return of the FTSE All World Technology industry. Power grid and storage investment of $479bn in 2025 16 will need to more or less double by 2030 (to around $900bn) to cope with increased demand, the integration of renewable energy and grid instability.

Figure 4: FTSE Global Nuclear Power Index 5yr performance (rebased, USD total return)

Source: FTSE Global Nuclear Power Index, data as at December 10, 2025. Past performance is not a guide to future returns. Please see the end for important legal disclosures.

Clean energy is also having an impact on traditional fossil fuel markets, such as oil. Electric vehicles reduced oil demand by ~1.3m barrels per day (bpd) in 2024, with the oil displacement from EVs expected to rise to 5m bpd 17 by 2030. This is having an important impact on currently weak oil demand and is raising concerns of a global oversupply.

Technology – plethora of positives and negatives

Artificial intelligence is the defining trend of the moment, dominating capital expenditure (capex), energy demand and financial market focus. In the US, AI-related capex recently accounted for around 5% of GDP 18. Global AI-related capex is expected to grow from $1.5 trillion in 2025 to $2.0 trillion in 2026 19.

This comes with significant additional energy and water demands, with the Lawrence Berkley National Laboratory estimating that data centres consumed 4.4% of electricity in the US, a figure that is forecast to grow to 12% by 2028 20. However, the actual carbon emissions from AI searches can vary dramatically between model, data centre, local grid and the time of day. Chip efficiencies are improving rapidly and tech companies are big buyers of renewable and nuclear power.

A recent US study 20 found that the carbon intensity of electricity used by data centres was 48% higher than the US average, partly because data centres are clustered in places with dirtier grids. 20 Looking ahead to 2026 and as data centre building continues, access to power is an increasingly critical focus for new projects. This will benefit both the most efficient data centres and renewable energy, particularly solar and storage, due to their low cost, speed and scalability of building. There is also an increasing focus on the potential returns of AI data centres. With energy and cooling one of the largest elements of operational expenditure (opex) 21 the need for efficiency throughout the AI and data centre value chains will only increase, both for emissions and cost/return reasons. As such, the continuing AI data centre build should boost demand for efficient IT products and low-carbon electricity.

Figure 5: Estimated opex of a 100MW data centre ($mn pa)

Source: Deconstructing the Data Center: A Look at the Cost Structure Igniting the AI Boom!

On the flip side to the concerns around the energy demands and associated emissions is the potential for AI applications to reduce emissions and solve sustainability challenges. A recent study finds that AI could reduce global emissions annually by 3.2 to 5.4 billion tonnes of CO2 (or equivalent) by 2035 22. If the solutions--which range from improving grid management to optimising processes for higher efficiency--are widely applied across geographies and industries, then emissions reductions could outweigh the additional power consumption from AI. There are also additional sustainability benefits which could come from advanced weather forecasting, improved SI data or accelerated drug development. 2026 will be a “show me” year for AI: it must demonstrate that it can manage power demands, efficiency, returns and deliver on the promised productivity benefits and emissions reductions.

Whilst AI remains in focus, other aspects of technology should not be overlooked by the sustainable investor in 2026.

Cybersecurity risks are growing and cyberattacks are increasing in number and becoming more sophisticated. The potential costs of cybercrime are estimated to be as high as $10.5 trillion 23, with significant impacts on consumers, corporates and governments.

Governance is increasingly complex for large technology companies, with multiple regulatory fines of over $1bn in 2024/25 24. Whilst such fines have so far had limited impact on the large free cash flows of big tech companies, they can have an impact on investors’ controversial conduct screens. Indeed, some tech companies already fail UN Global compact human rights rules due to their implementation of governments’ internet restrictions. Investors should keep an eye out for increasing geopolitical tensions and new challenges such as the debate over copyright in AI.

Figure 6: Active weight in ICB Technology for selected global SI indices (%)

Source: FTSE Russell. Data as at September 30, 2025. Past performance is not a guide to future returns. Please see the end for important legal disclosures.

A final risk for sustainable investors to be aware of is the exposure of sustainable investment funds to the tech sector. The sector has surged in performance over the last 5 years and sustainable investment funds can be overweight due to the sector’s strong ESG scores and low carbon intensity. FTSE Russell global sustainable investment indices are between 1- 8% overweight in technology stocks compared to market capitalisation-weighted benchmarks and any tech underperformance, as seen in 2022, could be painful.

Asia – it’s happening here

The epicentre for climate and sustainability activity is shifting to Asia, despite the region being a small part of the currently measured sustainable investment fund market.

It is responsible for around 60% of global carbon emissions 25 but is also home to the largest climate finance flows (43%) 26 and the majority of both the manufacturing and the installations and sales of renewable energy 27 and electric vehicles 28. Asia also faces some of the largest physical risks from climate change, with heatwaves in India, flooding in Bangladesh and cyclones in China. South Asia is also experiencing severe air pollution issues in urban areas, similar to those experienced by China in the early 2000s, which had a direct influence on the country’s shift towards a leading global position in clean energy.

Figure 7: Climate finance flows by region (2023, $bn)

Source: Global Landscape of Climate Finance 2025 - CPI.

China is key to the Asian market. Whilst it is the world’s largest carbon emitter, the country’s rate of emissions growth has started to reduce and the recent NDC3.0 pledge, shortly before COP30, of a 7-10% emissions cut from peak levels by 2035 has the potential to materially alter the direction of emissions globally. China is also the dominant player in the global clean energy market 29. In 2024 it produced 4 out of 5 solar panels and batteries, two thirds of electric vehicles and over half of heat pumps globally 30. Its exports are now over $20bn per month and they have particularly grown to the emerging markets, stimulating growth in renewables and EVs in these markets.

The huge capacity China has built up in clean energy, which is already sufficient to produce sufficient solar panels by 2030 to meet a 1.5 degree temperature rise trajectory, has also driven the dramatic price reductions in the renewables market. Across the solar, wind and batteries segments, prices have fallen by between 60% and 90% and in 2024 91% of the new solar and wind projects commissioned were cheaper than the cheapest available fossil fuel alternative 31.

This cost advantage will be critical going into 2026 and an increasing portion of the renewables market will shift from subsidies/feed-in tariffs to more price-sensitive auction and private contracts, including in China itself. However, the intense competition and falling prices have significantly impacted the profitability of Chinese renewable energy manufacturers, with solar producers operating at a loss in 2025. China’s anti-involution measures in 2026 are aimed at reducing the intensity of competition, which may slow price cuts but be positive for the returns of companies in the sector. China is also the biggest domestic market for clean energy products: EVs were 52% of new vehicle sales in August 2025 and in H1 2025 China installed twice as many solar panels as the rest of the world combined 32.

India is also becoming the potential new swing factor in global emissions. As the world’s most populous country, whilst it has relatively low emissions per capita it is also ranked third by total emissions. With a combination of rapid economic growth, increasing access to electricity and the increasing use of air conditioning (at least in part due to physical climate change) India is expected to see the largest energy demand growth to 2035 33. With 78% of electricity generation currently coming from fossil fuels 34 and the country estimated to be the largest source of oil demand (and the second largest for coal) by 2035, India will have a major impact on global emissions. However, India has pledged to achieve net zero emissions by 2070 and had installed 200GW of renewable energy by the end of 2024 (with a target of 500GW by 2030), when it is estimated it could make up to 40% of electricity generation 35. Coal is making up a smaller part of India’s overall electricity demand growth and even saw a small fall in H1 2025. The country is also planning to implement a carbon market for selected industries in 2026 and has plans to grow both nuclear power and biofuels. Whichever path India takes to power its growth--fossil energy or clean energy--the country is likely to have a significant impact on the global climate.

Figure 8: Indian electricity generation by source (TWh)

Source: World Energy Outlook 2025 – Analysis - IEA; Stated Policies Scenario.

Important sustainability-related activity is also happening in other Asian countries. Japan is implementing an often overlooked (but very large, estimated at around $1trn) GX energy transition plan to reduce emissions, stimulate green innovation, boost industrial competitiveness through increased efficiency and improve energy security 36. Singapore is also developing itself as a centre for sustainable investment with new climate disclosure rules and growing green revenues 37.

Beyond climate: large SDGs, on which millions of lives depend, are seeing change and opportunity

The Health Care and Food Producers sectors are not just essential—they are transformative for populations and economies. For investors aligning with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), the challenge is to capture upside from innovation while hedging systemic risks from inflation, inequality and climate volatility.

Health Care: a defensive sector under pressure, but poised for transformation

Global Health Care, estimated at $10 trillion 38, is larger than the green economy in terms of global market size, but it has been the third worst performing ICB Industry over the last 5 years. The sector continues to see numerous headwinds, such as pricing pressure, regulatory uncertainty and profitability challenges in hospitals and care. However, multiple sources of innovation provide opportunities and demographics make it an increasingly important sector for the economy, investors and people’s lives.

Cost Inflation and Fiscal Strain 39: Health Care spending accounts for a major share of government budgets--around 10% of GDP globally--and is seeing rapid inflation. Indebted governments face mounting pressure to contain spending, creating opportunities for cost- saving technologies such as telehealth and AI-driven diagnostics.

Figure 9: Public Health Care spending as % of GDP

Source: Healthcare Spending - Our World in Data.

Access and Equity Challenges: The rising cost of living and unequal access to care remain systemic risks, particularly in emerging markets (EM). These dynamics are catalysing demand for generics and low-cost delivery models.

Geographic Growth Drivers: Emerging Market demand is accelerating, supported by China’s Health Care expansion and rising middle-class consumption. Health security concerns post-pandemic have reinforced investment in resilient supply chains and domestic production. India and China are the leading producers of generic drugs and active ingredients 40 on which global Health Care relies, and they are increasingly important in drug discovery. Globalisation pressures could raise issues of “health security” 41.

Innovation Tailwinds:

GLP-1 Drugs: One in eight adults in the US has used GLP-1 therapies for diabetes or obesity 42. The market is projected to exceed $130 billion by 2030, with key patents starting to expire in 2026 43, paving the way for generics and oral formulations that could democratise access.

AI in Drug Discovery: Over $60 billion has flowed into AI-driven biotech. While no AI-discovered drug has yet received FDA approval, the technology is shortening R&D cycles as well as speeding up diagnosis 44.

Gene Therapies: The global gene therapy market is growing rapidly. Despite high costs and regulatory complexity, approvals are accelerating, including CRISPR-based treatments for rare diseases.

Digital Health and Telemedicine: Driven by cost and capacity issues, telehealth adoption and remote monitoring are expanding, creating investable opportunities in platforms, cybersecurity and data analytics.

Food Producers: essential, underperforming and facing structural shocks

Global food and agriculture is a multi-trillion-dollar market, yet millions remain food insecure. It is at the epicentre of the challenges presented by physical climate change, water stress and nature/biodiversity. It is also a key element of the economic cost of living crisis, with food inflation running at twice that of CPI 45. These pressures, which are not expected to significantly abate in 2026, have made Consumer Staples the worst-performing ICB Industry over 5 years. However, innovation, which is often sustainability-related, as well as the sector’s geopolitically essential nature, create opportunities.

Figure 10: FAO Global real food price index

Source: FAO Food Price Index | Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations.

Food Inflation and Security: Climate volatility, geopolitical tensions and fertilizer/fuel price shocks are driving systemic risk. Nearly 300 million people face acute hunger, while food inflation erodes purchasing power and shifts diets toward lower-quality calories.

Climate and Water Stress: Agriculture accounts for 70% of global freshwater withdrawals. Water scarcity and extreme weather could cut staple crop yields by up to 7% per degree of warming, creating ripple effects across supply chains and financial markets 46,47.

Technology Disruption: Agri-tech (biologicals, precision and data driven farming), food tech (AI-driven supply chains, alternative proteins), water-resilient agriculture, circular economy models (waste-to-value) and sustainable packaging 48,49 are all disruptive forces.
