data.title: Broadcom: Buy The Dip Or Regret It Forever
data.description:
On Dec. 11, 2025, Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) reported its fourth-quarter and full-year 2025 earnings. Revenue for the quarter hit $18.02 billion, outpacing estimates by 3.18%. Meanwhile, adjusted EPS came in at $1.95, which also topped forecasts by 4.27%.

For the full year, revenue reached $63.89 billion, exceeding what analysts expected by 0.86%, while annual adjusted EPS landed at $6.82, beating the consensus by 1.07%. That said, despite the seemingly positive figures, the stock has dropped about 13% since earnings.

But as I had a closer look, this decline appears driven by broader concerns about AI spending sustainability and not any real problems within the business. For this reason, and the ones outlined in this analysis, my Strong Buy rating for Broadcom remains in place.

What’s With Broadcom’s Fourth Quarter and Full-Year Financials?

For the fourth quarter, Broadcom’s revenue rose 28% to ~$18 billion compared to the same period last year, driven by strong demand from business segments:

Segments

Q4 ‘24

Q4 ‘25

YOY Change

Semiconductor solutions

$8,230

$11,072

35%

Infrastructure software

$5,824

$6,943

19%

Total

$14,054

$18,015

28%

In millions (USD$).

This demand is driven by Broadcom’s custom chips called XPUs, and hyperscalers like Alphabet (GOOGL/GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META) utilize these chips in data centers. In fact, Broadcom secured five major hyperscaler customers. One of which is Anthropic (ANTHRO), who placed a $10 billion order and an additional $11 billion follow-on, and another customer added a $1 billion order. These are committed purchase orders that are yet to materialize, and when they do, we can expect higher top-line figures.

In terms of profitability, gross profit rose 29% to $13.8 billion, while gross margin (GAAP) rose 0.8 percentage points to 76.61%. This means the company is keeping a larger portion of its revenue compared to the same period last year. On that note, a nearly 77% gross margin for a predominantly hardware company is impressive. Reminds me of Nvidia (NVDA).

As a result, operating income for the quarter jumped 53% to $7.7 billion, while net income nearly doubled at 97% to $8.5 billion, or $1.80 per share (basic). Ideally, you’d prefer to see the bottom line growing faster than the top line, as it means better operating leverage.

The annual figures look more impressive. Revenue rose 24% to $63.8 billion, gross profit expanded 27% to $49.4 billion, while gross margin expanded over two percentage points to 77.33%. That reflects pricing power. Meanwhile, operating income rose 62% to $25.2 billion, but the standout figure is Broadcom’s bottom line, exploding 292% to $23.1 billion year-over-year, or $4.91 per share (basic). Despite the average revenue growth, profit growth was significant, which tells us that the business is working at peak efficiency.

Broadcom’s balance sheet also looks pretty solid. Cash and cash equivalents reached $16.2 billion, while its total assets stood at $171 billion against total liabilities of $90 billion. This means Broadcom’s got plenty of financial flexibility; it could invest in R&D, make acquisitions, or even return value to shareholders through buybacks or dividends. Either way, Broadcom’s financials appear healthy.

For the next quarter (Q1’26), management guided revenue to roughly $19.1 billion with adjusted EBITDA of 67%. Considering its current financials, backlogs, and overall industry momentum, I think the target isn’t hard to surpass.

How is Broadcom Stock Performing?

At the time of this publication, the stock trades at around $352 per share following the post-earnings decline. That’s roughly 13% lower than its peak somewhere around Dec. 10, 2025.

The selling pressure came from macroeconomic fears about whether big tech companies can sustain their AI spending levels. That said, when you look at the bigger picture, Broadcom’s stock is still up around 56% over the past year, around 683% over the past 5 years, and a staggering 2,700% over the past 10 years.

In terms of valuation, the stock certainly looks quite expensive at first glance. Forward P/E (GAAP) sits at roughly 44x, which is about 38% higher than the sector median of ~32x.

These figures make sense. Broadcom is in a unique spot as one of the primary alternatives to Nvidia - Nvidia chips lean towards general-purpose utilization, while Broadcom’s XPUs are highly coveted for specific AI workloads.

What Could Push Broadcom Stock Even Higher?

Now that the stock has dipped following earnings, there are several factors that could catapult the company even higher. The most immediate one being…

Growth Driven by AI Semiconductor Demand

In the fourth quarter, CEO Hock Tan stated that AI semiconductor revenue increased 74% compared to the same period last year, and they expect this momentum to accelerate even more. For the first quarter of 2026, management is guiding $8.2 billion in revenue from AI semiconductors.

Moreover, the company has secured 5 major partnerships with hyperscalers, including Alphabet, Amazon, Microsoft, and Meta, which all operate massive cloud infrastructure networks with billions of users around the world.

However, the biggest news here is the additional $11 billion order from Anthropic - on top of the $10 billion in custom XPU chips they ordered during the third quarter of 2025. Again, these eXtreme Processing Units (XPUs) are designed specifically for each unique customer’s AI workload.

According to reports, XPUs offer better performance for specific workloads with lower costs compared to Nvidia’s chips- this is what positioned Broadcom as a leading alternative to Nvidia.

How will this affect Broadcom’s financials moving forward? During the earnings call, management stated that the fourth quarter gross margin came in at nearly 78%. For now, let’s use that as a basis. If AI revenue hits $8.2 billion as guided with a 78% gross margin, this segment alone would generate roughly $6.4 billion in gross profit.

After R&D and all the administrative costs, which typically run around 50%, we’d expect ~$4.1 billion in operating income from just AI semiconductors in a single quarter, or over $16 billion in annualized terms. That would be a significant growth driver for the company.

Broadcom is a Top Player in the Custom Chip Market

Next quarter’s expected $8.2 billion in AI revenue would make up around 43% of the total revenue, should it materialize as expected. I can see these numbers only climbing further as hyperscalers reduce their dependence on a single chip supplier, and Broadcom’s XPUs position the company as a viable alternative for Alphabet, Meta, OpenAI (OPENAI), and others who are also trying to reduce their dependence.

Remember, Broadcom’s approach is different from Nvidia's. Nvidia provided general-purpose GPUs that can handle different kinds of AI tasks but aren’t optimized for any specific job. On the other hand, Broadcom’s XPUs can be specialized for specific tasks. For example, Alphabet’s TPUs and Meta's MTIA chips are byproducts of Broadcom’s expertise.

If custom chip revenue reaches $33 billion annually with 77 to 78% gross margins, that’s roughly $25.4 billion in gross profit for this segment alone.

Broadcom’s VMware Software Continues to Bring in Steady Revenue

As you might’ve also noticed, Broadcom’s AI semiconductors grab all the attention. However, the company’s software infrastructure has also become a pillar for financial stability.

In 2025, the segment’s revenue rose 26% to $27 billion with gross margins around 93%. That’s exceptional. Revenue from this segment balances the cyclical nature of semiconductor sales, since software infrastructure provides stable and high-margin sales. For investors, parallel growth from this segment creates a moat that pure-play chip companies can’t offer.

Software operating margin also reached 78% from 72% when VMware operated independently - it moved through two phases of integration. Phase 1 moved the biggest 10,000 customers from perpetual licenses to subscriptions. This means paying once and owning forever. Over 90% of the customers have switched to subscription-based payments, providing a predictable revenue stream for the company.

The second phase of the integration is happening now, and this focuses on getting customers to use the VCF 9.0 platform for traditional applications.

Going back, I think this software infrastructure performance deserves more attention. If this segment reaches $30 billion in annual revenue by 2027 with a 93% gross margin and 80% operating margin, this segment alone would bring roughly $24 billion in operating income and would meaningfully contribute to Broadcom’s bottom-line figures.

Generally speaking, stock prices move at the same clip as the earnings. If these figures were to materialize, we can expect great things from AVGO in 2026.

What Are the Risks Around Broadcom?

Nothing is without risks, and Broadcom is no exception. The company’s most immediate risks right now are…

A Valuation That Leans Towards the Expensive Side

After gaining in 2025, the stock still trades at valuations with notably high expectations for future growth.

As covered earlier, the company’s forward P/E (GAAP) sits at ~45x, and this leaves very little room for disappointment. For these reasons, future gains might be limited if actual results fail to catch up with these expectations, even if the business performs well in absolute terms. As we know, the market shoots first before asking questions.

Although I’m not really concerned about it, the recent 13% drop since earnings despite strong results can help illustrate these risks. When stocks trade at higher multiples, they need perfect execution. Although most of the sell-off was triggered by macroeconomic fears, potential gross margin pressures from AI semiconductors added fuel to the fire.

If growth slows down, maybe AI revenue grows 50% annually instead of doubling, and software revenue grows high single digits. 2027 EPS might reach $12 to $13, and that would put the stock at roughly 26 to 28x fiscal 2027 earnings at current prices. The point is, share price appreciation could be limited as the business grows into valuation, especially if economic factors slow AI spending.

Competition Within the Custom Chip Market is Heating Up

Another risk that can’t be ignored is competition. Recently, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) announced plans for its Instinct MI400 series launching in 2026. Like Broadcom, I believe AMD is also one of the most viable alternatives to Nvidia for specific AI workload requirements. It also managed to secure partnerships from hyperscalers like OpenAI, Meta, Microsoft, and others for its newest chips.

Beyond AMD, Nvidia itself maintains a solid competitive moat through its CUDA software platform and mature AI infrastructure ecosystem. Sure, custom chips excel at specific workloads they’re designed for, but Nvidia’s general-purpose GPUs still offer unmatched benefit from years of development- well, you get what you pay for, right?

Other well-funded startups are also exploring entirely new chip architectures like wafer-scale engines or neuromorphic chips that can mimic brain structures. Most importantly, there’s optical computing that uses light instead of electricity. Although certainly not in the near future, these might disrupt the market if they achieve commercial viability.

But I think the biggest threat comes from customers developing their own chips in-house. If hyperscalers get better at designing their own silicon, like what Google is doing with its TPUs or Amazon with its Trainium, they might need less help from external sources over time. If that materializes, pricing pressure might squeeze gross margins notably.

Broadcom’s Customer Base is Highly Concentrated

Here’s another risk. Broadcom has a $73 billion backlog of AI chip orders. Many investors point to this news, and rightfully so, but the downside is that this entire pile of future revenue comes from basically five big companies: Alphabet, Meta, ByteDance, Anthropic, and OpenAI.

These are committed revenues, so once they materialize, investors can expect solid earnings improvement considering Broadcom’s over 70% gross margins. And in an ideal world, share price moves in step with earnings- this is the scenario I want to unfold. For example, if you’re running a restaurant and 80% of your revenue comes from five regular customers, you’re not really a restaurant but a private kitchen for five people, aren’t you? As investors, we need to see growth beyond the backlog.

I think that’s Broadcom’s position in the AI, at least right now. I could play devil's advocate and argue that custom AI chip adoption is still in early stages, and that hyperscalers are the initial wave of customers, and that AI infrastructure companies will soon reinforce their Nvidia racks with custom AI chips for specific AI workloads, but most of that scenario is still in hopes & dreams- far from the foreseeable future.

In terms of earnings, if even one of these customers hits the brakes, Broadcom’s figures will be impacted…severely. Say Alphabet decides to delay TPU deployment by three months as management rethinks its data center approach. That single decision could knock billions of dollars off Broadcom’s quarterly revenue.

Long-term investors might not be overly concerned about this matter, but it’s critical for us to understand that Broadcom’s quarterly results might experience notable volatility due to external factors, and they won’t necessarily reflect the company’s execution.

Verdict: Is Broadcom Stock a Buy in 2026?

I’ve been watching the AI infrastructure space closely, and one thing that’s been very obvious- at least to me, is that many of us are so focused on whoever builds the fastest chip. I say an equally compelling comparison between customization and commoditization is being overlooked.

Hyperscalers weren’t born yesterday. They know that betting everything on one supplier is dangerous, regardless of how good that supplier ecosystem might seem. This is largely the reason why they’re expanding from off-the-shelf solutions to purpose-built hardware that does exactly what needs to be done.

Well, it also makes economic sense. For those spending billions on building data centers, even just 10% efficiency gains from specialized hardware means hundreds of millions in annual savings, and that’s necessary when competing with tech giants with nearly unlimited funding- in fact, even the same tech giants are expanding towards specialized hardware, mainly for the same purpose.

This is why I think Broadcom’s business model stood out. It holds an oligopoly where only two entities can translate customer requirements into production silicon at scale, and only very few companies can do this reliably.

Combine that with its financial performance, balance sheet, and overall market position, makes the recent price decline an attractive entry point for investors. This, and for all the reasons mentioned above, my Strong Buy rating remains in place.

data.title: Why This 5% Drop In Qualcomm Is A Gift For Patient Investors
data.description:
Introduction

Qualcomm Incorporated (QCOM) is not a stock known for volatility; it is a stable semiconductor company and a dividend payer. Yet, we saw the stock plunge almost 5% in a single session yesterday, on Monday, January 12th, a relatively rare move for a company of this size and stability.

Interestingly, this sell-off happened on the news, which is not really news. The catalyst was a downgrade from Mizuho, which cut its rating from “Outperform” to “Neutral” and pulled down its price target from $200 down to $175. The reason was the handset headwinds, specifically, that Qualcomm is expected to lose market share in Apple devices this year and next because Apple (AAPL) is transitioning to its own modems.

Now, if you have been following Qualcomm, you might be confused right now. This “breaking news” that sent the stock down is something the market had been discussing for some time now. The valuation of QCOM reflects this negativity, and the company has been working throughout 2025 on diversifying away from mobile devices.

So for the long-term investor, this disconnect between the market’s memory and the company’s execution creates an interesting setup. We are approaching earnings with a relatively low valuation multiple, but this stock is known for plunging on earnings even further. Could it be that we will be able to “catch” Qualcomm at the historically low valuation a few weeks from now? I think it’s possible!

The “Apple Headwind”

I have to open this piece with the Apple business because I am seriously confused. The bearish thesis says Qualcomm is losing Apple as a client, and it’s really a reason to worry, because until 2025, Apple relied on Qualcomm entirely, and as you can imagine, it was an enormous part of Qualcomm's revenues. The problem is that the news about Apple rolling out its C1 came out almost a year ago, or to be precise, on February 19, 2025.

While this had been a valid reason for concern, Qualcomm’s management has been very transparent about what is going on and what they are planning to do with this. Even at the recent UBS Global Technology Conference, the CEO of the company explicitly stated that they have been planning their business assuming that this customer is going to go away in 2027. They have built their financial model and their diversification strategy on the assumption that Apple's revenue goes to zero. Is it upsetting? Definitely. But it’s not a good enough reason to re-rate this stock now, at mid-January 2026. The management said not once they are preparing for that revenue to disappear, but then, an analyst repeats the same thing, and the stock drops 5%.

We have been planning our business, assuming that this customer [Apple] is going to go away in '27. I think that's our planning assumption. That's how we've been managing the company. That's how we're accelerating our diversification. We had provided metrics, which we're very pleased with it, how the non-Apple business in the company is growing. It showed that our strategy is working, our engine is working, and we're going to keep doing that.

I hope you see how the market is effectively double-counting the risk. It penalized the stock when the news first broke and then penalized it again now as the timeline approaches, despite the fact that the company has successfully navigated the transition period better than expected.

While the Apple-Qualcomm relationship is coming to an end, the rest of the handset business is doing well. Even in a flat global market, Qualcomm’s Android business (excluding Apple) grew revenues by about 10% over the trailing 12 months. They are seeing an expansion in the premium tier of smartphones, where they dominate.

You have an expansion of the premium and the high tier, you have a low-end market. The middle is contracting. I think if you remember back when I became Chief Executive Officer, I think I said in 2021, I said our strategy in phones is going to be very simple. We're going to go after share of wallet, we're going to concentrate on technology leadership in premium and high on Android, and we significantly increased the Op margin of the business because the premium tier is more resilient, I think, to the cost of technology.

This success is driven by technological dominance. For example, their Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 mobile platform has the world’s fastest mobile CPU, the Oryon, with significant improvements in single-core and multi-core performance. They are delivering 40% better power efficiency and massive AI processing gains on the handset, and this is why Samsung and Xiaomi stick to Qualcomm regardless of what Apple does.

Cars, Robots, and Data Centers

As I already said, Qualcomm is actively working on diversifying away from being just a smartphone chip company. It is entering the automotive and even data center sectors.

The most tangible success they show is in Automotive. In Fiscal 2025, Qualcomm’s QCT Automotive revenues surged 36% year-over-year to nearly $4 billion. In the fourth quarter alone, they exceeded the $1 billion revenue milestone, up 17% from the previous year.

They are partnering with major automakers, and I have a separate article about one of them. This is the Snapdragon Ride Pilot with BMW, which is a full-system solution for automated driving that is already installed in vehicles like the BMW iX3. They sell a whole platform with hardware and software. As vehicles transition to "Software-Defined Vehicles," Qualcomm is positioning itself as the foundational architecture for the entire automotive industry, competing mostly with Mobileye (MBLY) and Nvidia (NVDA) but with a unique advantage in connectivity and low-power processing.

And then there is the data center business. So if Nvidia builds the massive chips that teach AI models how to think, Qualcomm is using those models to answer actual questions, which is called inference.

And I think what's happening is we see this as a one of the entry point for us, as AI is going to go into inference, you're going to be able to build large inference-focused clusters, the data center is going to go to the next phase of this aggregation. You're going to have dedicated hardware tooling inference. And we think that creates an opportunity, I think, for us to enter. We expect there's going to be competition, everybody is playing to win.

At the UBS conference, they already announced a serious deal—200 megawatts of capacity with a Saudi AI company that is going to use Qualcomm’s new AI 200 and AI 250 systems.

I think the first customer that we have is 200 megawatts of data center with the Saudi national AI company. We're very pleased with the progress they made with the license and everything that's moving to execution. We are in conversations, as you would imagine, with all the hyperscalers.

They say that as AI grows, the most important factor will not be who has the biggest capacity but who uses energy the best. And this makes sense because data centers are already running out of power and struggling to stay cool. Qualcomm has decades of experience in figuring out how to get maximum performance out of a battery without burning a hole in the device. If they can focus on bringing the same efficiency to the data center and steal even a tiny piece of market share from the enterprises, it would be a massive win that the current stock price isn't accounting for at all.

Financials

Since 2016, Qualcomm’s revenue has expanded by nearly 90%. That is a compounded annual growth rate of roughly 7.5%. So the narrative about the company that slowly loses its edge is irrelevant here.

However, looking at the Return on Invested Capital (ROIC), you can see that this company is trying hard to rotate into new sectors. A few years ago, around 2021 and 2022, Qualcomm had an impressive ROIC of over 40%, which would be among the top companies on the market. Today, that number is down significantly, at around 13.5%.

Qualcomm is transitioning from a business model heavily reliant on licensing (which has extremely high margins and requires very little capital) to a business model that involves making actual physical products for cars/robots/data centers in new industries. This may continue weighing on both the ROIC and even margins, but as long as this metric is at least 5-6% higher than the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC), the company succeeds in adding value with its investments.

We are about two weeks out from earnings, and there is a pattern here. If you look at the last eight quarters, Qualcomm has beaten estimates on both earnings and revenue every single time. Right now, the market expects revenue around $12.16 billion and normalized earnings per share of $3.40.

The problem is that beating the numbers doesn't seem to help the stock price in the short term. Every time in the last year, the company posts great results, and the stock drops anyway. After the last report, even though the results were fine, shares fell from around $180 to $160.

Valuation

Right now, Qualcomm trades at a forward Price-to-Earnings, or P/E, ratio of roughly 14x. In a tech sector where AI companies trade at 30x, 40x, or even more earnings, Qualcomm is really cheap. It is trading at a discount not just to its semiconductor peers but to its own five-year historical average. The market is pricing Qualcomm as if the Apple exit will crush them, assigning almost zero value to the $4 billion automotive business and all of the other wins with the PC sector, mobile Android, etc.

The funny thing is that while you wait for the market to wake up, Qualcomm pays you, and not a little. In Fiscal 2025, they generated $12.8 billion in free cash flow, and they returned a nice part of it to the shareholders. They returned $3.4 billion, including $1 billion in dividends, and bought back their own stock for $2.4 billion.

Conclusion

The recent 5% drop with this Mizuho downgrade is a weird piece of news. Qualcomm is aggressively building the future. Qualcomm is not going to double in price overnight, as it is a mature, complex company in the middle of a difficult transition. At 14x earnings, with a 36% growing automotive business and a management team that is aggressively returning cash to shareholders, the risk-reward ratio looks in investors' favor.

Opening a full-sized position before the earnings may not be the best idea, as the stock has a history of post-earnings drops, even when beating the results. I would be dividing the capital I want to allocate and buying slightly on this dip, but also leaving some away to add on the next weakness.

data.title: PDI Continues To Generate Yields That Exceed 14% Heading Into Favorable Conditions
data.description:
The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) has been one of my favorite income-producing assets over the past several years. Despite a difficult macroeconomic environment when inflation ran to the high single digits and the Fed increased interest rates to their highest level in more than a decade, PDI continued to deliver reliable monthly income. I believe that 2026 is setting up as a favorable year for multi-sector fixed income, which would be bullish for PDI. PDI utilizes a dynamic strategy that targets immediate income as its primary objective by allocating assets across global credit sectors while incorporating leverage to enhance its distributable cash flow. For investors looking for recurring income, PDI’s structure is ideal because the distributions are paid on a monthly basis, which makes matching cash flow from investments to their monthly expenses easier. We’re entering an easing cycle where I believe PDI’s structure and the current macro backdrop present an opportunity for capital appreciation and double-digit yields to be produced from this investment. The underlying assets in PDI’s portfolio have been discounted, and I believe the underlying value of its holdings will increase while these investments continue generating the same amount of income in the future. I am bullish on PDI, and I am continuing to add to my position.

Following Up on My Previous Article About PDI

Back in August I had written an article on PDI (can be read here) where I discussed how I was still bullish heading into a continued rate-cutting cycle. Fed Chair Powell had signaled that several cuts could occur at the last 3 FOMC meetings in 2025, which I felt could boost PDI’s NAV and share price. PDI's resilient monthly distribution and strong management made it a compelling income-producing asset heading into a lower rate environment. The management team had done a good job at managing risk from its investments and leverage utilization and created a proven track record of distributing income to its investors. I am following up with a new article now that we received 3 additional rate cuts in 2025 to discuss why I am still bullish and adding to my position in PDI in 2026.

How PDI Works and Why I Believe There is Value to Be Unlocked

PDI is a closed-end fund (CEF), which is different from an open-ended mutual fund or ETF. One of the biggest things to understand is that the share price is driven by the net asset value (NAV) of its underlying holdings and that the share price can diverge in either direction from the NAV. CEFs have fixed share counts and trade intraday, which means that the market price per share and NAV always need to be evaluated. On 1/9/26, PDI had a NAV of $16.97 and a share price of $18.20, which means that PDI was trading at a 7.25% premium to its actual NAV. This matters for income investors because buying at a premium can reduce your forward return if the premium compresses.

PDI looks to generate its recurring income through a dynamic asset allocation across multiple fixed-income sectors. PDI predominantly focuses on corporate debt, mortgage-related and other asset-backed securities, government and sovereign debt, and a mix of fixed- and floating-rate investments. The U.S. market is its primary focus, as over 70% of its investments are in the U.S. The reason why I like their dynamic asset allocation rather than a single-sector strategy is that as rates head lower, it should reshape the yield curve, which could cause spreads to move with growth expectations. PDI has the flexibility to rotate assets across mortgages, credit, and securitized products, which gives them a structural advantage. PDI’s mandate includes mortgage-related and asset-backed exposure, which could be very lucrative in 2026. As volatility in interest rates subsides, areas in securitized credit can see improved liquidity and demand.

CME Group is projecting that the most likely scenario is that we see 2 rate cuts in 2026. There is a foundational relationship between price and interest rates in the bond market, as they traditionally move inversely. Credit bonds, securitized credit, and mortgage-related assets can utilize fixed rates. Bonds can be looked at as contracts that pay a fixed amount of cash flow with periodic coupon payments and a principal that is repaid when the bond matures. The current market value of the bond is the present value of cash flow discounted at the current market interest rate. When interest rates fall, the discount rate, which is applied to the bond's cash flow, will decline as well, which causes the present value of the bond to rise along with its price. PDI has been investing in assets throughout the high-rate environment, and when bonds are issued when rates are high, the coupon is locked in at a higher level. As rates decline, new bonds are issued with lower coupons and yields, which means that investors will pay more for the older bonds with higher coupons because they are generating a larger stream of income. The market price for the old bonds will rise until the yields align with newer bonds being issued.

I believe that as rates continue to decline in value, a large portion of PDI’s portfolio will appreciate in value. Interest rates were 5% or higher for a year and a half over the past 3 years. PDI was actively investing in new bonds during this period, and for most of this period, interest rates exceeded 4%. If a bond has a $1,000 face value that pays a 5% coupon, which is $50 per year, and the market yield falls to 3.5%, investors will bid up the bonds with a 5% coupon until the yields match. This is because nobody is going to pay $1,000 for $35 of income if they can get the same bond that was issued the prior year for $1,000 that’s generating $50. Rates were over 4% for roughly 3 years and I believe there is value to be unlocked in PDI’s asset base. PDI is in a situation where it can generate NAV expansion and price appreciation over time as the Fed takes rates lower.

PDI Has Generated Reliable Income Since Its Inception, Which is Bullish for Lower Rates

I believe that income investors are going to look for new ways to manufacture income in 2026. The Fed has cut rates by 175 bps since August of 2024, as they have fallen from 5.5% to 3.75%. There is still $7.77 trillion sitting in money market accounts on the sidelines, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. As rates continue to decline, there will be less of a reason for investors to utilize money markets to manufacture income. This figure also doesn't show the amount of capital allocated toward CDs and short-duration bonds. I believe that as rates fall closer to 3% and then under, investors will no longer be enticed by the risk-free rate of return and will look toward the equity markets to generate income.

PDI has been a solid income investment for many years. Since its inception, the distribution has increased from $0.18 to $0.23 without falling. Long-term investors have generated $42.50 of distribution income since the summer of 2012, which is 170% of the initial share price of $25. Investors have benefited from recurring monthly income no matter what has happened in the market. Today shares of PDI are generating $2.65 in distributed income, which is a 14.54% yield. I think that PDI is going to see more inflows, as this is a distribution that has been sustainable through many different economic environments. On an annualized basis, shares of PDI are down -27.2%, but when the distribution yield is factored in, there is a total return of 142.80%, which works out to an average annualized return of roughly 10.58%. I think that this is a great opportunity to lock in the 14.54% yield, as I believe PDI’s NAV will increase in value, pushing the share price higher.

Risks to Investing in PDI

While I am bullish on PDI, there are several risk factors that investors should consider. PDI is a leveraged CEF, which makes it sensitive to interest rates, credit events, and macroeconomic shifts. If, for some reason, something occurs in the data to make the Fed change their stance on monetary policy, it could be very negative for PDI as it could lead to declines in bond prices and the underlying NAV. PDI employs a leverage ratio that exceeds 30%, and in a higher-rate environment, the chances of its underlying holdings experiencing higher rates of defaults or credit downgrades increase significantly. If companies in their portfolio experience hardships, then PDI’s distribution could face coverage shortfalls as some of its assets could turn illiquid and disrupt the strong distribution yield. Investors should do their own due diligence, as it’s difficult to research PDI’s underlying assets, and a lot of faith is being placed on the management team. Investors should also be aware that PDI focuses on fixed income, and its share price is unlikely to replicate the returns of an S&P 500 index fund.

Conclusion

The Fed is cutting rates, and we have a new Fed Chair starting in May. The prediction markets have Kevin Warsh and Kevin Hasset as most likely to succeed Fed Chair Powell at 45% and 41%. I think that either of them would be very bullish for a lower rate environment, which would be excellent for PDI. As rates fall, I believe that more income investors will look toward CEFs such as PDI to generate income because, in addition to a long track record of generating income, they also have the ability to watch their underlying investments increase in value. My prediction is that we get 3 rate cuts in 2026 and 2 rate cuts in 2027, which would get rates to around 2.25%. In this type of environment, PDI’s assets should appreciate in value considering all of the investments they made during the higher rate environment. I am adding to PDI because I believe that the NAV is compressed and will expand by a good margin while generating recurring monthly income, which I am able to lock in at 14.54%. I am long PDI heading into a more dovish Fed and monetary policy.

data.title: LiveWire: Unprofitable, With No Clear Catalysts
data.description:
Investment Thesis

LiveWire (LVWR) designs and manufactures electric motorcycles and bikes. It was spun off from Harley Davidson held roughly 89% share as of December 2024 (p. 87).

Despite Harley-Davidson's (HOG) support, LiveWire products don't seem to be a good fit for the market today, given the slow adoption of e-motorcycles. Sales are modest, and LiveWire has yet to achieve breakeven. With EV transition policies and incentives waning in the US and the European Union, combined with stiff competition from Chinese suppliers, LiveWire's prospects appear speculative.

Portfolio and Market Position

LiveWire's e-motorcycle portfolio consists of 4 models. The motorcycles are fashionable, and thanks to multiple price cuts (as discussed below), they're priced competitively in the premium in-city mobility market.

The market share of e-motorcycles remains modest in the US. In the case of LiveWire, there appears to be a product/market-fit problem. Leisure motorcycling is an important aspect that doesn't appear adequately addressed by LiveWire. The in-city range is short, ranging between 113 and 146 miles. The power of the motor (63 kW–75 kW) and acceleration speed give a desired thrill. On the flip side, this power means the batteries drain fast, especially on highways where the powerful motors are most useful. When they're in sports mode or going on a highway at 55 miles per hour, the range drops significantly. For example, the Del Mar, which carries an in-city range of 113 miles, will see its range drop to 70 miles on a highway traveling 55 miles per hour.

Some industry pundits express satisfaction over how LiveWire motorcycles feel, with some describing a "heartbeat" sensation (some sort of haptic feedback) when twisting the throttle (video timestamp: 6:30). This emphasis on the rider's experience is one of the manifestations of the cultural links between LiveWire and its parent, Harley-Davidson. However, unlike Harley-Davidson, which has a clear lead in the premium motorcycle market, LiveWire has yet to show spec superiority. For example, the Can-Am Pulse, which has a city range of 100 miles and a 35 kW motor, sells for $11,000. The S2 Mulholland, which has a 120-mile in-city range and carries a 63 kW motor, sells for $12,000. Another strong contender is Zero Motorcycles, which has more models than LiveWire, with motor sizes ranging from 8.4 kW to 75 kW and a broader range of in-city options, from 70 miles to 179 miles, and a price range between $4,400 and $23,000.

The company also sells electric bikes under the STACYC brand. Marketing materials on the STACYC website suggest an emphasis on the kids' recreational market, as opposed to motorway-grade transport.

Revenue Trends

LiveWire sales stood at $5.7 million in Q3'25, up 28.3% YoY. The growth is primarily attributed to the STACYC segment, which contributed $4.7 million to total sales (or 82.4%), after the segment's sales grew by 46%. These sales dynamics are critical and could change the entire investment thesis from a leading premium, automotive-grade electric motorcycle supplier to an e-bike player focused on kids and youth sports and entertainment.

The Q3'25 unit sales performance reinforces the premise that there might be a product/market mismatch with LiveWire's e-motorcycle portfolio. E-motorcycle units sold in Q3'25 stood at 184 units, up from 99 units in the same period of the prior year. However, this unit volume increase came on the back of steep price cuts as part of the Twist & Go promotion, announced in August 2025 and lasting until mid-December 2025. The promotion provided price cuts on certain models within the e-motorcycle segment.

While unit sales increased 85.9% from 99 units in the three months ended September 30, 2024 to 184 units in the three months ended September 30, 2025, this increase was offset by new incentives announced during the third quarter of 2025, including the Twist & Go Promotion. These incentives resulted in decreases to revenue of $1,086 thousand related to unit sales in the three months ended September 30, 2025. LiveWire Q3'25 SEC Filings

An important point here is that part of the sales decline in Q3'25 is attributed to the company adjusting for past sales to extend the promotion on dealers' inventory. This means that sales to dealers had to be adjusted, and this adjustment occurred in Q3'25. This puts the decline in the e-motorcycle sales segment into perspective. The e-motorcycle sales segment stood at $970,000 (p. 23), down 19.8% from $1.2 million reported in Q3'24, despite unit sales going up. At first glance, this suggests that the Twist & Go promotional price cuts were ineffective, failing to boost sales. This is not the case. In Q3'25, LiveWire recorded about $1 million in "variable consideration related to previous revenue," which is the revenue to dealers who are now sitting on inventory but paid the full price previously. This means that the revenue from units sold in Q3'25 was closer to $2 million, an increase from the $1.2 million reported in the same period of 2024.

The Company offers sales incentive programs to independent dealers, distributors and retail customers designed to promote the sale of its products. The Company estimates its variable consideration related to its sales incentive programs using the expected value method. The Company accounts for consideration payable as part of its sales incentives as a reduction of revenue, which is accrued at the later of the date the related sale is recorded or the date the incentive program is both approved and communicated. Variable consideration related to sales incentives and rights to return is adjusted at the earliest of when the amount of consideration the Company expects to receive changes, or the consideration becomes fixed. [...] As a result of these incentives in 2025, for the three and nine months ended September 30, 2025, the Company recorded $982 thousand and $897 thousand, respectively, for variable consideration related to previously recognized sales. LiveWire Q3'25 SEC Filings (p.14).

Earnings Trends

In addition to the quality of revenue (being concentrated in the e-bike segment), LiveWire is yet to reach breakeven. Even when adjusting for the contra-revenue "Variable Consideration" amount, the company still had negative gross margins in Q3'25, suggesting the existence of high fixed costs that require larger volumes to break even. GAAP revenue stood at $5.7 million, while COGS stood at $8.6 million in Q3'25. Adding $982,000 to adjust for the contra-sales portion (p.14) brings adjusted revenue to $6.7 million, which is still below the $8.6 million COGS incurred during the period.

Management expressed a new emphasis on reducing cash burn and costs during Q3'25, with results mirroring this renewed commitment. SG&A and engineering expenses were down to $15.9 million from $25 million in the same period of the prior year. But it increases the risks of LiveWire under-investing in the business, as discussed below.

Balance Sheet and Cash Burn

LiveWire's cash is dwindling fast. As of September 2025, the company had $16.3 million in cash and equivalents, down from $64.3 million at the start of the year. The company's property, plant, and equipment "PPE" declined from $34.0 million to $28.5 million, a manifestation of the company's cash-preservation mode and the renewed emphasis on cost management, raising a risk of asset decay, as CapEx declined from $6.7 million to $2.7 million in the nine months ended September 2025. Despite the cost-reduction programs, the company's current assets don't cover the cash burn rate. In the nine months ended September 2025, the company's operating cash outflows stood at $45.7 million, which, when annualized, exceeded the current assets, which stood at $46.2 million. This means that the company will likely need to raise cash or debt to keep the lights on. In August 2025, LiveWire filed a registration of $100 million (p. 10) worth of shares with the SEC, which is a signal of a potential dilution going into 2026.

Valuation

At a market cap of $611 million and TTM sales of $25 million, LiveWire's TTM P/S ratio of 24.4x seems to incorporate a growth premium when juxtaposed against a P/S ratio of its parent, Harley Davidson, whose P/S ratio stands at 0.6x. This comparison is particularly useful, given that both companies share many operational and manufacturing functions, as per their contract manufacturing and joint development agreements (p. 20).

The company also seems overvalued when compared to the sector's median of a 1x P/S ratio.

With dilution and/or leverage most likely ahead of LiveWire and the dismal profitability and revenue trends, the company's valuation seems overoptimistic.

The EV transition seems to have also provided an opportunity to new market entrants, especially in China, capitalizing on this technological reset to build their position in the engine and powertrain industries, once dominated by US and European companies. This is also true for new companies emerging in the US, vying for market share in this emerging market. Zero Motorcycles, one of LiveWire's main competitors, was formed by a former NASA engineer in 2006.

Final Thoughts and Risks

LiveWire benefits from the commercial and technical support from its parent company, Harley-Davidson, the esteemed premium motorcycle leader with unmatched brand strength. However, the prospects of LiveWire as an investment seem fragile. Revenue growth seems to be driven by e-bikes used mainly by younger customers. Although the revenue contribution from the e-bike segment is welcomed and could provide growth going forward, it is detrimental to the valuation narrative. E-bikes don't require the automotive verification and certification required by motorcycles, decreasing the barriers to entry and, by extension, the valuation premium enjoyed by motorway vehicles.

The main risk to this sell hypothesis is a rebound in the EV market or a better-than-expected performance of the e-bike segment. The market is nascent, and, while EV adoption has slowed, the secular trend is arguably still alive.
